title: Bar types and why imbalance / run bars exist
author: DayNo4131
selftext:
Like most people, I started with time bars. After the ~100k-th ML model that overfit beautifully in-sample and died out-of-sample, I started looking for sampling schemes that reduce overfitting upstream, not downstream.

That’s when I first came across event-based bars in Marcos López de Prado’s book.

Here’s the rough hierarchy.

1. Time bars (1m, 5m, 1h, etc.)
Bars are sampled on the clock, regardless of market activity.

Pros:
- Simple
- Familiar

Cons:
- Mix low- and high-activity periods
- Volatility clustering dominates
- ML models mostly learn *when* you sampled, not *what* happened

2. Volume / dollar bars
Bars close after a fixed amount of volume or notional.

Pros:
- Better activity normalization
- Less dead time

Cons:
- Still blind to direction
- Large trades dominate
- Volatility is reduced, not controlled

3. Tick bars
Bars close after a fixed number of trades.

Pros:
- Removes time entirely
- Better aligned with microstructure

Cons:
- Treats all trades equally
- No notion of pressure or imbalance

4. Imbalance bars (tick / volume / dollar)
Bars close when order-flow imbalance exceeds an adaptive threshold.

Pros:
- Sample when information arrives
- Bar frequency adapts to market stress
- Volatility is implicitly normalized
- Returns become closer to homoskedastic

This was the first time I felt the sampling itself was working with the model instead of against it.

5. Run bars (natural extension)
Run bars condition not just on imbalance magnitude, but on persistent directional runs.

Why they matter:
- Capture order-flow persistence
- Filter out chop
- Align bars with genuine pressure
- Further reduce the need for downstream volatility modeling

In practice, many people who adopt imbalance bars eventually end up with run bars, because they push signal into microstructure dynamics instead of price noise.

Why imbalance / run bars help with overfitting
They address the volatility problem at the sampling layer.

Once bars adapt to information flow:
- Volatility modeling becomes less critical
- Features are more stable
- Labels behave better
- ML models have fewer degrees of freedom to exploit

This is exactly why they resonated with me after repeatedly running into overfitting with standard bar constructions.

TL;DR
- Time bars sample the clock
- Volume/tick bars sample activity
- Imbalance bars sample information
- Run bars sample persistent information

If your models keep overfitting, changing the sampling scheme often helps more than changing the model.

Question:
Are there other event-based bar constructions people have found useful for reducing model overfitting, beyond imbalance and run bars?

comments.author: happydaayz
comments.body: Which charting software has run bars?

replies.author: DayNo4131
replies.body:
Run bars are usually not offered as a packaged product.  
In most cases, you have to implement them yourself.

Some professional platforms like NinjaTrader and Sierra Chart *can* support run-bar–style logic via custom bar types, but they don’t ship them as a standard chart option. You’re effectively writing the sampler.

On the Python side, mlfinlab is the main library that implements imbalance and run bars out of the box.

That said, iterating tick-by-tick in Python is slow at scale.  
I ended up coding my own run bars in Rust, where streaming over every tick is fast and deterministic, then exporting OHLCV for analysis / charting.
